text:
Summarize (Orchestrating the Discussion)

Discussing Solutions and Strategies

Have students present and discuss their strategies for answering the questions. Focus the students on the equivalent ratio and unit rate relationships.

Suggested Questions

With each strategy in the Initial Challenge, have students justify why the strategy works. Then ask students to connect how the different strategies represent the same type of thinking.

  • Whose strategy (Brandon's, Sofia's, or Kaedon's) is most like how you thought about answering the questions about the smoothie bars?
  • Is there one strategy you prefer over the others? Why?
  • How do the strategies compare?
  • How did you fill in your rate table for What If..? Situation A?
  • How is finding equivalent ratios helpful to answer questions?
  • How does finding the quantities on the table help you think about how to write the equation for the cost in What If..? Situation A?

• Answers will vary.
  • Answers will vary. All the strategies work and provide different levels of efficiency. It would be interesting to note if students become flexible at using different strategies at different times.
  • Students should notice that all three strategies are related to ratio relationships. Sofia is scaling the ratios. Brandon scales to find a unit rate that is easily scaled to other quantities. Kaedon represent the unit rate algebraically. He can then use that expression or equation to find other equivalent quantities.
  • Some students might discuss making jumps (multiplicative reasoning using factors and multiples to find equivalent ratios). Some students might discuss finding the unit rate and scaling the unit rate to find equivalent ratios.
  • Finding different amounts of the variables answers the questions. You can use equivalent ratios to find equivalent variable amounts.
  • The repeated reasoning in the table helps to find the pattern that can be expressed as a rule. That rule can be written as an equation.

Making the Mathematics Explicit

Students may fill out entries in the table by using the unit rate $8 per 1 box without really understanding what the quotient 48/6 means. Encourage them to explain what the "8" means. (Language)

Suggested Questions

  • If I take the $48 as the amount of money collected and divide it by the 6 boxes of bars, I get 8. What does that 8 mean? How would I explain to someone what that quantity is in the comparison?
Students need to explain scaling the ratio down to find the cost for 1 box, the unit rate for 1 box.

• How would you write a comparison statement
  • How would this help me find the amount collected for ant number of boxes of bars?
  • How could we write this as an equation?

• For every 1 box of smoothie bars, they collect $8
  • You would take the number of boxes and multiply by 8 to find the amount collected.
  • Number of boxes times 8 equals the amount collected, B × 8 = A

Students may need to revisit some of the thinking from Variables and Patterns to consider what a graph of the relationship might look like. You might sketch or have students sketch the graph during the discussion.

  • What would you have to think about when making the graph for What If...? Situation A?

Answers might include the following: What are the variables? Which one is independent/dependent? How will I have the axes count like a number line? What numbers make sense on the number lines—should I count by 1 or some other amount?

Once you have reviewed what the graph might look like, help students to connect the different representation of ratios, unit rate, equations, and graphs.

  • What connections do you see between the unit rate, the ratio, and the equation you wrote?
  • How would these connections be shown in a graph?

• Students might say that the unit rate is a scaled-down version of the other ratios in the table. The unit rate also appears in the equation connecting cost and number because the equation tells you to multiply the number of bars by the unit price, or cost per 1 bar.
  • Students might say that the unit rate can be seen inthe relationship between variables. As the number of boxes increases by 1, the cost increases by $8. And 8 is the unit rate for one box.

Note that the number of boxes would not be sold in parts. Someone is going to buy a whole box, not a part of one. So the points on the graph should not be connected. The data in between the points would not make sense in this context.

  • What strategy did you use to answer What If...? Situation C (2)?
  • What strategy did you use to answer What If...? Situation C (3)?
  • What connections do you see between the unit rate, the ratio, and the equation in What If...? Situation C?

• Answers will vary. Some students may use the equation and multiply 0.75 by 10; some students may make a rate table to find the cost for 10; some students may guess and check; some students may use a graph.
  • Answers will vary. Some students may use the equation and divide $9 by 0.75; some students may make a rate table to find the number of smoothie bars for $9; some students may guess and check; some students may use a graph.
  • Students might say that the unit rate is how much 1 smoothie bar costs. The unit rate also appears in the equation connecting cost and number because the equation tells you to multiply the number of bars by the unit price, or cost per 1 smoothie bar.

Now What Do Students Know?

Ask students to reflect on the discussion and answer the Now What Do You Know? questions.

See answers to the problem for more information about the strategies and embedded mathematics.

Reflecting on Student Learning

  • Use the following questions to assess student understanding at the end of the lesson.
    • What evidence do I have that students understand the Now What Do You Know? question?
      • Where did my students get stuck?
      • What strategies did they use?
      • What breakthroughs did my students have today?
    • How will I use this to plan for tomorrow? For the next time I teach this lesson?
    • Where will I have the opportunity to reinforce these ideas as I continue through this unit? The next unit?
